  • stats — Statistics »

The Statistics class¶

class gmaopy.stats.statistics.Statistics(object)¶
missing_value = 1.7E38

The official missing value used by SemperPy for statistics

classmethod register(category, name, what)¶
Parameters:
  • category (str) – the are different groups of statistics for different applications, to date, there is obsstat for observation (impact) statistics, score for forecast skill scores, product for statistics on analyses
  • name (string) – name of the statistic. This is the official name which will be used by the user
  • what (class) – A class to instanciate to implement the statistics named name

Registers classes to implement different statistics. Once a class is registered, its name is known by the system and it can be stored in a database.

Note :class method
classmethod create(category, name)¶
Parameters:
  • category (str) – the category under which we are working,
  • name (string) – name of the statistic to instanticiate

Instanciates the class corresponding to that name in the given category. If an instance of that class has been created earlier, it is returned (singleton), this speeds up the process.

Warning :statistics classes are not supposed to store attributes.
Note :class method
data2np(data, index)¶
Parameters:
  • data (list) – the data as passed to the compute method
  • index (int) – index in the tuple

The data is sent to the compute methods as represented by the SQL client, it is a list of tuple, each tuple contains the value of each column described in the select statement. By default, this tuple is: level,variable,statistic,date,value,count,expver.

This method returns a numpy array containing all the values in the list of value number index in the tuple. It slices data vertically, for example:

data = [
    (500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,4.5,34,'expver1'),
    (500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,40.5,134,'expver2'),
    (500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,14.5,354,'expver3'),
    (500.0,'t','sum',2012010100,379.5,2500,'expver3'),
]
print s.data2np(data,4)
numpy array: [4.5,40.5,14.5,379.5]
Note :utility method
split(data, index)¶
Parameters:
  • data (list) – the data as passed to the compute method
  • index (int) – index in the tuple

This method splits the list of data into as many lists as different values of column number index is found and returns a dictionary with each list. For example:

data = [
    (500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,4.5,34,'expver1'),
    (500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,40.5,134,'expver2'),
    (500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,14.5,354,'expver3'),
    (500.0,'t','sum',2012010100,379.5,2500,'expver3'),
]
print s.splitData(data,6)
{
    'expver1': [(500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,4.5,34,'expver1')],
    'expver2': [(500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,40.5,134,'expver2')],
    'expver3': [(500.0,'h','sum',2012010100,14.5,354,'expver3'),
                (500.0,'t','sum',2012010100,379.5,2500,'expver3')]
}

This method can be called as many times as needed on results from previous call

Note :utility method
store(storer, record, **kargs)¶
Parameters:
  • storer (hidden) – the storer passed to the compute method
  • record (dict) – the record data passed to the compute method
  • kargs – values to insert in the record before storing it

This method is also a utility function, it stores data:

s.stor(storer,record,value=24.3,count=2000)
Note :utility method
array(value)¶
Parameters:value (float) – the value to store in an array

This utility method builds a masked array of one value, this is useful to return the final floating point value after aggregating statistics from a database.

Note :utility method
requirements_raw()¶

This method must return the name of a variable to be extracted from the raw file, variable for gridded field or variable for ODS files. By default it returns [‘var’] which means whichever variable the user specified. However, sometimes it can get a little more complicated. To compute the normalized cost we need whichever variable the user specified (oma or omf) and xvec, in that case this method should:

def requirements_raw(self):
    return ['var','xvec']
Note :information method

To compute the estimared sigo, we want to ignore the variable the user specified because we need two variables, oma and omf. In that case this method should:

def requirements_raw(self):
        return ['oma','omf']
requirements_db()¶

This method is only used when computing statistics from the database. By default it returns the name of the statistic computed by this object. The final name of a statistic (e.g. rms) is often different from the name of the statistic stored in the database which is used to compute it (e.g. sumsquare). This method should return the name of the statistic to fetch from the database. It can also return the name of statistics for a particular variable (e.g. [‘sumsquare:omf’]. If you specify that you want to compute the root mean square (rms), the system uses this method to determine which statistic(s) from raw data to generate. When plotting, the system calls this method to determine which partial statistic(s) to retrieve from the database, for rms:

def requirements_db():
    return 'sumsquare'
Note :information method
unit()¶

This method must either return None (no specific unit) or a unit for that statistic. By default None is returned and the variable is used to determine the unit (e.g. rms of meters is meters). If the statistic has a unit, like for example a coefficient (%), it should be returned here and it will be used instead of the unit from the variable.

Note :information method
moreIsBetter()¶

This method is needed when plotting to determine sometimes how data needs to be presented. By default it return False (e.g. rms, the higher the worse). This enables to orient colorbars in a consistent way.

Note :information method
columns()¶

This method returns an empty list by default. If a statistic requires more columns from the database than returned by default, it can add here the extra required column names.

Note :information method

..automethod:: prepareProcessing

param what:the name of the plot being processed (its class)
type what:str

This method is called once only at the very beginning of computing statistics to give objects a chance to initialise data, or open files etc...

..automethod:: completeProcessing

param what:the name of the plot being processed (its class)
type what:str

This method is called once only at the very end of computing statistics to give objects a chance to close files, normalize data etc...

name()¶

This method must return the official name of this statistic

Note :abstract method must be overwritten
compute_raw(variables, name, storer, record)¶
Parameters:
  • variables (dict) – a dictionary of masked arrays for each variable requested for that statistic
  • name (str) – name of the variable which was specified by the user
  • storer – an object in charge of storing data
  • record (dict) – a record containing all the information about the statistic we are about to compute

This method is called when the data specified by the user has been extracted from the raw data. At this stage, the variable record was filled with all the details to describe the piece of data (expver, date, level, etc...), this method is supposed to compute and fill in two keywords:

  • value
  • count (could be optional)

Please see examples in Add your own statistic for more information.

compute_db(columns, data, name)¶
Parameters:
  • columns (dict) – a dictionary indexed by the name of columns form the database, containing an index in the tuple representing the data
  • data (dict) – A dictionanry of list of tuples containing data from the database, please see data2np()
  • name (str) – name of the variable which was specified by the user

This method is called when the data specified by the user has been extracted from database and aggregated according to the user’s specifications.

Please see examples in Add your own statistic for more information.
